one_line_takeaway: First empirical census of ~1,899 open-source MCP servers: healthy activity metrics, but 7.2% carry conventional vulnerabilities (credential exposure leading at 3.6%) and 5.5% exhibit MCP-specific tool poisoning that static analyzers miss, motivating dedicated MCP security tooling and registry governance.
key_finding: MCP servers look healthy but carry both conventional and MCP-specific security debt. Health (RQ-0): they exceed traditional-software baselines on activity and social reach but lag slightly on issue resolution. Security (RQ-1): SonarQube found 7.2% of servers affected by 8 distinct vulnerability patterns (credential exposure most prevalent at 3.6%), only 3 of which overlap with known ecosystem vulnerabilities; separately, mcp-scan found 5.5% of servers suffer from MCP-specific tool poisoning that the static analyzer entirely misses — showing traditional tools capture only part of the risk landscape. Maintainability (RQ-2): 66% of servers have critical/blocker-level code smells (high cognitive complexity most common) and 14.4% have critical/blocker-level bugs, with a derived taxonomy of ten code smells and nine bug patterns that largely mirror traditional software. Takeaway: MCP needs dedicated vulnerability-detection tooling and governance (e.g., MCP-specific entries in vulnerability databases, automated scanning in registries) while still benefiting from conventional static analysis and refactoring.
core_contribution: The first large-scale empirical study of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) server ecosystem, assessing health/sustainability, security vulnerabilities, and maintainability across a curated dataset of 1,899 open-source MCP servers. The authors' own contributions are: (1) the curated dataset (88 official + 255 community + 1,556 GitHub-mined servers); (2) a set of cross-ecosystem comparative baselines normalized from PyPI, NPM, IaC and ML software, compiled via a structured literature review of 40 prior studies; (3) a hybrid analysis pipeline that pairs a general-purpose static analyzer (SonarQube) with an MCP-specific dynamic scanner (mcp-scan), which they claim is the first framework to jointly evaluate traditional and MCP-specific issues in one pipeline; and (4) the empirical findings themselves. Note: the tool-poisoning threat concept and the mcp-scan tool are prior/third-party work (Invariant Labs), not this paper's invention — the paper measures the real-world prevalence of these issues rather than proposing the attack or the scanner.
threat_model: The paper is a measurement study, not an attack paper, but it situates its security analysis within the MCP-specific threat surface drawn from prior work: because MCP servers expose tools to a foundation model via reflected natural-language tool descriptions (docstrings/schemas), an adversary can embed malicious instructions in a tool's description (tool poisoning) to steer an FM into misusing an otherwise-legitimate tool — e.g., redirecting a WhatsApp send_reply to an attacker proxy number to exfiltrate chat history — without tampering with executable code, dependencies, or control flow. Related MCP-specific threats it references (from cited work) include MCP rug pulls (post-approval modification of tool descriptions), prompt injection via descriptions, cross-origin escalation across untrusted servers, and demonstrated exploits (Malicious Code Execution, Remote Access Control, Credential Theft, Retrieval-Agent Deception). The paper's contribution is quantifying how often such issues actually occur in public servers, alongside conventional code-level vulnerabilities.
limitations: The >=10-star filter reduced the security/maintainability analysis from 1,899 to 583 servers, biasing toward more-popular repos and away from the long tail. MCP-specific scanning covered only 73 servers (from an n=83 sample), so the 5.5% tool-poisoning figure rests on a small, credential-gated sample and inherits mcp-scan's proprietary, description-only detection (it cannot see source, config, or access-control artifacts, and can miss or over-flag). Detection is tool-bounded on both sides: SonarQube's rule set and mcp-scan's pattern matching define what counts as a 'vulnerability,' and prevalence numbers are lower bounds. Pattern taxonomies depend on an LLM-Jury whose validation used only 75 samples. Cross-ecosystem baselines are assembled from heterogeneous prior studies with differing definitions and tools, so comparisons (e.g., 5.5 vs 2.5 commits/week) are indicative rather than strictly like-for-like. The dataset is a Mar-Jun 2025 snapshot of a fast-moving ecosystem. The study reports prevalence, not exploitability or real-world impact of the flagged issues.

datasets_used: Authors' curated MCP server dataset: 1,899 servers total (88 official + 255 community from Anthropic's repo, + 1,556 GitHub-mined via SDK-import search)
Filtered analysis subset of 583 MCP servers (>=10 GitHub stars) used for SonarQube static analysis
Random sample of 83 servers (Cochran's formula, 95% CI, 10% margin) for mcp-scan; 73 successfully scanned
Language distribution of the 583 (Table 1): TypeScript 227, Python 196, JavaScript 115, Others 45
40 prior studies selected (from 135 Google Scholar searches) as cross-ecosystem baselines (PyPI, NPM, IaC, ML)
quantitative_results: RQ-0 health: 5.5 commits/week (vs 2.5 traditional); CI adoption 42.2% (vs 40.3%); median contributor count 2.0 (vs 2.0); median contributor follower count 129.6 (vs 37.3); median issue resolution 5.6 days (vs 4.0)
RQ-1 static: 7.2% of servers affected by 8 distinct vulnerability patterns; credential exposure most prevalent at 3.6%; only 3 of 8 overlap with known ecosystem vulnerabilities
RQ-1 MCP-specific: 5.5% of scanned servers exhibit tool poisoning (via mcp-scan), undetected by SonarQube
RQ-2 maintainability: 66% of servers have critical/blocker code smells (high cognitive complexity most prevalent); 14.4% have critical/blocker bugs; taxonomy of 10 code smells and 9 bug patterns
LLM-Jury validation: Fleiss' kappa = 1.0 (vulnerabilities), 0.9 (code smells and bugs); consistency over k=5 runs: 100% cluster-membership, 0.75 mean naming similarity
mcp-scan run: 60/83 scanned initially, +13 after maintainer patch = 73 total; SonarQube scan time 30s-5min per repo, all 583 done in ~4 days on an M3 MacBook Air
baselines_compared: Traditional/general OSS software metrics (health, sustainability, vulnerability, code-smell prevalence) aggregated from prior literature
Tooling: SonarQube static analyzer (chosen over FindBugs and PMD) paired with mcp-scan (chosen over the unmaintained mcpSafetyScanner)
